coredata.dc:title: Bell pepper derived nitrogen-doped carbon dots as a pH-modulated fluorescence switching sensor with high sensitivity for visual sensing of 4-nitrophenol
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Guoqiang
Li, Tingting
Liu, Ziyi
Luo, Xinyu
Zhang, Ting
Tang, Siyuan
Wang, Xu
Chen, Da
coredata.dc:description: Recently, converting bio-waste into bio-asset and implementing a portable sensing instrument for pollutant monitoring has been highly desirable and challenging. Herein, biomass-derived nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDs) are prepared hydrothermally and emit blue fluorescence (470 nm) with a high quantum yield of 23.2%. Significantly, CDs can serve as a pH-modulated fluorescence switching nano-sensor to detect 4-NP from 0.054 to 68 μM with low detection limit (LOD, 54 nM) and limit of quantification (LOQ, 181 nM) based on inner filter effect. Moreover, the satisfactory recovery of 101.8–107.5% is gained in practical sample monitoring. Furthermore, a smartphone-integrated optosensing device with CDs-based film is developed for detecting 4-NP with LOD and LOQ of 0.110 μM and 0.350 μM. Concomitantly, the practicability of this device is further validated in several crop samples with satisfactory recovery rates of 101.6–108.6%. Therefore, this work provides a reliable way and a prospective application for on-site 4-NP monitoring in food.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bell pepper
4-Nitrophenol sensing
pH-modulated fluorescence switching
Optosensing system
Visual sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Portable smartphone-assisted devices for visual 4-NP detection
Fluorescence characteristics of the as-prepared CDs
Fluorescence response of CDs for 4-NP detection
Mechanism of pH-modulated fluorescence switching detection system
CDs/PVA nanocomposite and smartphone-assisted analysis device

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: K C Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by projects from National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (No. 61604084 ) and K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University. The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis absorption, PLE and PL spectra of CDs. Inserts: the optical images of CDs under daylight (left) and 365 UV light (right). (b) 3D mapping of PLE and PL wavelengths of CDs. (c) The summary of PL wavelengths and FQYs of biomass-derived CDs obtained in previous reports and our work. Influences of pH value (d), NaCl concentration (e) and irradiation time under UV light (f) to CDs fluorescence state, respectively (n = 5).

(a) The 3D excitation-emission matrix mapping of CDs in the presence of 4-NP at pH = 5, 7 and 9. (b) UV–vis absorbance of 4-NP at different pH level. (c) Fluorescence intensities of CDs in the buffer of different pH in the presence and absence of 4-NP. (d) Reversibility of the fluorescence nano-sensor modulated by pH level. (e) Schematic diagram of the principle of the pH-modulated fluorescence switching behavior.
Schematic illustration of smartphone-assisted analysis device for application in 4-NP sensing.
Illustration of the preparation process of CDs (a), the preparation flow chart of CDs-based composite film (b) and the practical application in on-site 4-NP sensing (c).
Determination of 4-NP in practical samples using fluorescence and HPLC techniques (n = 5).

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Tabulated t-value at the 95% confidence level is 2.78.

head.ce:title: Bell pepper derived nitrogen-doped carbon dots as a pH-modulated fluorescence switching sensor with high sensitivity for visual sensing of 4-nitrophenol

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microelectronic Science and Engineering, School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, PR China

School of Physical Science and Technology

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering
Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Full Spectral Solar Electricity Generation (FSSEG)
Southern University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Full Spectral Solar Electricity Generation (FSSEG), Southern University of Science and Technology, Shenzhen 518055, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, converting bio-waste into bio-asset and implementing a portable sensing instrument for pollutant monitoring has been highly desirable and challenging. Herein, biomass-derived nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDs) are prepared hydrothermally and emit blue fluorescence (470 nm) with a high quantum yield of 23.2%. Significantly, CDs can serve as a pH-modulated fluorescence switching nano-sensor to detect 4-NP from 0.054 to 68 μM with low detection limit (LOD, 54 nM) and limit of quantification (LOQ, 181 nM) based on inner filter effect. Moreover, the satisfactory recovery of 101.8–107.5% is gained in practical sample monitoring. Furthermore, a smartphone-integrated optosensing device with CDs-based film is developed for detecting 4-NP with LOD and LOQ of 0.110 μM and 0.350 μM. Concomitantly, the practicability of this device is further validated in several crop samples with satisfactory recovery rates of 101.6–108.6%. Therefore, this work provides a reliable way and a prospective application for on-site 4-NP monitoring in food.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As an important and standard nitrophenol compound, 4-ntrophenol (4-NP) is widely employed in the fields of pharmaceutical manufactures and fine chemicals (Shen et al., 2017; Yue et al., 2018). Due to its highly toxic, the constant release of 4-NP in soils and aquatic environments limits the pollutant-free drinking water. Meanwhile, resulting groundwater pollution has seriously damaged the ecological environment and threatened human health, leading to the strong stimulation of skin, eyes and human organs (mutagenic effects in mammals and cyto-embryotoxic) (Ray et al., 2022). Moreover, 4-NP is also a kind of hard-to-degrade pollutant in environment, which readily enters the food chain to accumulate in animals and food products (Jiang & Liu, 2020; Zhu et al., 2022). The United States Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA) lists it as a “priority pollutant” and establishes an allowable maximum standard (0.43 µM) of 4-NP for drinking water (Wei et al., 2011). Hence, designs of an effective sensing method for precise observation of 4-NP residues in food environment are an increasing demand.

Over the years, certain advanced techniques have been employed in determination of 4-NP, including photoelectrochemistry, liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), etc (Kitanovski et al., 2012; Mehdinia et al., 2014; Pang et al., 2021). Despite these methods have their own merits and give various insights for 4-NP determination, they have some inherent drawbacks, such as prolonged analysis time, skilled personnel and elaborate sample preparation procedure, necessitating researchers to develop more straightforward and more effective methodologies to detect 4-NP. In contrast, fluorescence spectroscopy shares the competitive advantages of quick responsiveness, convenience, high selectivity and sensitivity (Ci et al., 2023; Liu & Yang, 2022). Meanwhile, carbon dots (CDs), a new kind of luminescent nanoparticle materials with superior optical properties, less toxicity, chemical inertness, good biocompatibility and water solubility, have been demonstrated for constructing fluorescence probes (Cao et al., 2022; Russo et al., 2023). Recently, much attention has been paid to CDs derived from biomass or even biological waste because of its cheapness and accessibility (Wang & Chen, 2020). They are rich in carbon, nitrogen and oxygen elements and are easily converted into CDs with different surface functional groups and unique properties. Therefore, biomass-derived CDs can be easily modulated and controlled the physical or chemical properties. For instance, Jia et al. produced nitrogen-doped CDs hydrothermally with 1, 2-ethylenediamine and pitaya peel with a fluorescence quantum yield (FQY) of 5.6% and reported the application for tetracyclines detection in food samples (Jia & Chen, 2023). Similarly, Sun et al. utilized the catalpa walnut shells to synthesize CDs, and FQY was around 10%, which was used as a sensor to identify 5-nitroimidazole antibiotics (Sun et al., 2022). However, most biomass-derived CDs do not achieve high FQY (usually <10%) and excellent luminescence stability. It is known that heteroatom doping, such as nitrogen-doping, is regarded as a promising strategy to optimize the FQY because of electron withdrawing properties and high electronegativity of nitrogen atoms (Tang et al., 2022). In addition, in recent years, many researchers have devoted in establishing a portable, reliable and real-time pollutant monitoring technology, improving detection efficiency (Hao & Yang, 2022; Wang & Da, 2020). Simultaneously, the integration of smartphone in sensing devices is a significant simplification of designs, portability, versatility and reduced costs for the detecting system (Lu et al., 2022; Hao & Dong, 2022). Consequently, it is significant to develop a high FQY and stable photoluminescence biomass-derived CDs and construct a portable smartphone-assisted device to realize sensitive and visual real-time detection.

Herein, this work proposes a facile strategy to prepare environmentally sustainable, eco-friendly nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDs) using bell pepper as a precursor and further employs them to construct a smartphone-integrated sensing device for sensitive determination of 4-NP on the spot. From Scheme 1 , it can be observed that the bell pepper is a natural plant with a high nitrogen element content. Also, it contains a variety of vitamin C, folic acids and amino acids, which can serve as both carbon and nitrogen sources to prepare CDs. The as-synthesized CDs show a bright blue luminescence with excellent stability and a high FQY. More meaningfully, CDs can sensitively response to 4-NP in a wide concentration range with low detection limit (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ). In addition, the fluorescence recovery of CDs occurs as the pH level decreases. It is ascribed to an apparent blue shift of the 4-NP absorption peak which can restrict the IFE effect between CDs and 4-NP. In this manner, a pH-modulated fluorescence switching sensor is further developed for visual sensing of 4-NP by applying an RGB (Red, Green and Blue) analysis application. Therefore, our work not only makes a positive contribution to green synthesis of biomass CDs, but also develops a pH-modulated fluorescence switching nano-sensor with high sensitivity for visual 4-NP sensing in environmental water and crops.

The bell pepper was purchased from a local supermarket in Ningbo (Zhejiang, China). K2CO3 (99.99%), CaCl2 (99.99%), CoCl2 (99.99%), HgCl2 (99.99%), NiCl2 (99.99%), FeCl2 (99.99%), PbCl2 (99.99%), CuSO4 (99.99%), AgNO3 (99.99%), CdCl2 (99.99%), NaCl (99.99%), MnCl2 (99.99%), ZnCl2 (99.99%) and BaCl2 (99.99%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). 2-nitrophenol (2-NP), 3-nitrophenol (3-NP), 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) and all amino acids were acquired from Aladdin Chemistry Co. (Shanghai, China), Ltd. All reagents were used directly without further purification, and all solutions were dissolved in deionized water with 18.0 MΩ∙cm (Millipore Milli-Q system, USA).

The microstructures were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Joel/JEM-2100, Japan). The crystalline structure of CDs was recorded on an X-ray diffraction (XRD, D5005, SIEMENS, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Shimadu XRD-6000 powder, Kyoto, Japan) was performed to examine the surface chemistry of CDs. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of CDs was studied by a Nicolet FT-IR-iS5 spectrophotometer (Thermo, USA). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of CDs were recorded by UV-3101PC (Shimadzu, Japan). The photoluminescence excitation (PLE) and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CDs were recorded by the spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-7000 spectrophotometers, Tokyo, Japan). The fluorescence decay status and lifetimes were recorded in the Edinburgh FLS 1000 spectrometer (London, England).

The CDs were prepared hydrothermally with bell pepper as raw materials. Briefly, 10.0 g of bell pepper was crushed and dissolved in 40 mL of ultrapure water for 30 min stir at room temperature. And then, this solution was poured into a 100 mL autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. After cooled down to indoor temperature naturally, the products were centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 30 min. Further, a 0.22 µm polytetrafluoroethylene filter membrane was selected to purify the obtained brown solution to eliminate large aggregations. Finally, the resulting CDs were freeze-dried to obtain dark brown solids for use.

The FQY of CDs was measured using quinine sulfate (FQY = 54%, 0.1 M H2SO4) as a reference substance. The FQY of CDs was calculated through the following equation (Wang & Chen, 2021),
(1)
ϕ d
=
ϕ r
×
(
I d
/
I r
)
×
(
A r
/
A d
)
×
(
n d
/
n r
)
2

The ϕ stands for the quantum yield. Subscripts d and r refer to the unknown (CDs) and the standard (quinine sulfate), respectively. A is the absorbance, and n refers to the refractive index of solvent (1.33 for 0.1 M H2SO4). I is the integrated fluorescence emission intensity.

Typically, 4-NP solutions at different concentrations were mixed with CDs (120 mg‧L-1) followed by addition of the pH = 9 buffer solution to maintain a total volume of 3 mL. After being incubated for 1.5 min at indoor temperature, the PL spectra were monitored at 470 nm. Subsequently, for the sake of evaluating the selectivity of CDs, several potential interference substances (including K+, Ca2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ag+, Cd2+, Na+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, glutamic acid (Glu), threonine (Thr), histidine (His), homocysteine (Hcy), serine (Ser), arginine (Arg), valine (Val), glycine (Glv), leucine (Leu), 2-NP and 3-NP; 600 μM) were introduced instead of 4-NP (80 μM) for testing in a similar way. In quantitative measures, uncertainties arising from the balances, volumetric measuring devices, calibration curves, sample preparation and instrumental factors have been considered. For pH-dependency fluorescence switching experiments, HCl and NaOH solutions (1 M) were employed to modulate the pH of buffer solutions.

To evaluate the practicality of the established sensor for determination of 4-NP, three kinds of samples (tap water, wastewater and Yongjiang river water) were selected as samples for practical analysis. Samples of tap water and wastewater (both from Ningbo University, China) were collected in respective precleaned glass vessels. The 50 mL water samples were filtered through a 0.22 μm Millipore filter to eliminate impurities. Further, the treated samples were adequately mixed with 4-NP solutions of different content for analysis. Meanwhile, we used the HPLC assay as a reference method to verify the accuracy of our strategy, and combined the results of both methods for t-test analysis. The detection experiments via two methods were conducted five times, and the average values of the results were used in the subsequent analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Portable smartphone-assisted devices for visual 4-NP detection
ce:section.ce:para: For quick response and simplicity of visual 4-NP detection, a portable smartphone-assisted device was designed. Firstly, fluorescent CDs-based composite film was fabricated by mixing CDs into the non-luminescent polyvinyl alcohol (PVA). Then CDs solution was diluted with a pH = 9 buffer before fabrication. Subsequently, the CDs and PVA mixture were taken in a polytetrafluoroethylene plate for water evaporation at room temperature. Furthermore, the obtained CDs/PVA films were cut into small CDs/PVA starlike films with the same diameter. Then the tiny CDs/PVA starlike films were embedded in the castor oil-based waterborne polyurethane (CWPU) films modified by aminopropyltriethoxysilane (KH550). The obtained composite films were attached to the surface of practical samples (potatoes, cherry tomatoes and apples). After completely reacting with samples, the starlike films were taken out and provided to smartphone-assisted sensing platform for determining the 4-NP residue in practical samples.

The obtained starlike films were inserted into the portable smartphone-assisted sensing instrument to capture the fluorescence images under UV light. Then, the images were recognized and converted to RGB signals values by the smartphone’s application. The sum RGB values were calculated by the following equation,
(2)
Sum RGB values = R e d + G r e e n + B l u e

The morphology of the CDs is studied using a TEM experiment. As described in Fig. S1
(a), the synthesized CDs show nearly spherical nanoparticles of similar size, and the high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image in the inset discloses that CDs have a clear lattice structure with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which agrees with the (100) diffraction facet of graphite. The corresponding diameter distribution of CDs indicates that the size is in the range from 2 to 6 nm (Fig. S1 (b)). The powder XRD pattern of CDs displays a diffraction peak centered at about 22.6° ( Fig. S2 ), indicating the graphitic structure of the as-synthesized CDs (Bruno et al., 2022).

Surface functional groups and components of CDs are evaluated by FT-IR and XPS. As illustrated in Fig. S1 (c), the FT-IR spectrum reveals that the characteristic peaks ranging from 3187 to 3413 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of –OH/NH. The absorption bands at 1638 cm−1 and 1700 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of CC and CO, whereas the peak at 1391 cm−1 results from the tensile vibration of CH (Carneiro et al., 2019; Yan et al., 2020). Similarly, the band at 1090 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibrations of CN/CO, and the band at 1198 cm−1 is related to the CO stretching vibration (Chen et al., 2020). Fig. S3 shows the total XPS spectrum of the CDs, which exhibits three prominent peaks of C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s at 283.8 eV, 398.7 eV and 531.0 eV, respectively. This indicates that CDs are mainly composed of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen elements with atomic percentages of 70.77%, 4.75% and 24.48%. The high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. S1 (d)) reveals three peaks at 284.5 eV, 285.8 eV and 288.1 eV, corresponding to sp3 carbon (CC), sp3 carbon (CN) and sp2 carbon (CO/CN), respectively (Zhang & Chen, 2014). The high-resolution N 1 s XPS spectrum displays three peaks at 399.3 eV, 400.1 eV and 401.7 eV, indicating the presence of CNC, N(C)3 and CN groups (Fig. S1 (e)). Moreover, Fig. S1 (f) shows that two peaks at 531.6 eV and 533.0 eV, which belong to CO and CO on the CDs surface, respectively (Gu et al., 2018). The above analysis confirms that the abundance of amino acids and aromatic substances from bell pepper cause plentiful hydroxyl and carboxylic groups on the CDs surface, which is conducive to further applications in the biosystem.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence characteristics of the as-prepared CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of as-prepared CDs are systematically studied through the UV–vis absorption, fluorescence PLE and PL spectra measurements (Fig. 1 (a)). CDs exhibit three absorption areas at 230 nm, 281 nm and 310–420 nm. The characteristic absorption band at 230 nm is the π-π* electronic transition of the CC bond, whereas the peak at 281 nm can be appointed to the n-π* electronic transition of the CO functional group (Jiang & Qin, 2019). The weak and wide absorption band in the 310–420 nm range can be denoted as the N-related defect bonds or surface states (Wu et al., 2020). Under the excitation of 401 nm, CDs enable to emit a bright blue fluorescence at 470 nm. Accordingly, the CDs solution shows a pale-yellow color in a visible light, and a strong blue fluorescence can be observed under a 365 nm ultraviolet light. Moreover, the three-dimensional (3D) fluorescence map displays the strong blue fluorescence center of CDs in Fig. 1 (b). Surprisingly, the FQY of CDs is calculated to be about 23.2%, which is comparable with other biomass-derived CDs reported in the literature (Fig. 1 (c) and Table S1 ).

With the aim of applying CDs in practical sensing applications, a good fluorescence stability is quite necessary. Therefore, the photostability of CDs under various conditions is further investigated. As demonstrated in Fig. 1 (d), there is no apparent change in PL intensity of CDs in the pH range of 2–10, implying that CDs are not susceptible to pH fluctuations even under extremely acidic conditions. The saline solution (NaCl) at high concentration can impose no statistically significant difference in the PL intensity even as the concentration is increased to 2.5 mol‧L-1 (Fig. 1 (e)). In addition, the fluorescence intensity of CDs scarcely exhibits any variation under ultraviolet irradiation for 150 min (Fig. 1 (f)). These results confirm the excellent fluorescence stability of CDs, endowing the sensor’s capability for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of CDs for 4-NP detection
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to achieve the optical sensing performance toward 4-NP, a series of detection parameters including temperature, pH and incubation time of the reaction system are optimized. As shown in Fig. 2
(a), the PL intensity of the system nearly remains unchanged after a continuous heating equilibrium, indicating the sensing system is very stable even at a temperature of 60 °C. Unexpectedly, the quenching efficiency increases in response to pH and almost reaches the maximum when the pH level is 9 (Fig. 2 (b)). It is reflected that the response of CDs toward 4-NP is easily modulated by the pH value. Meanwhile, considering the excellent fluorescence stability of CDs with the pH ranging from 2 to 10, pH = 9 is chosen as the optimal condition for sensing 4-NP. Finally, the reaction time of the sensor to the target 4-NP is studied. The result in Fig. 2 (c) shows that the response of CDs to 4-NP tends to stabilize after 2 min. Overall, these experiment conditions are beneficial for practical detection on the spot.

Subsequently, taking into account the complexity of the practical environmental system, an excellent fluorescence sensor should not only show high sensitivity but also exhibit a specific selectivity to analyte. Hence, some potential interference substances (including K+, Ca2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Ag+, Cd2+, Na+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Glu, Thr, His, Hcy, Ser, Arg, Val, Glv and Leu) and nitrophenol compounds (2-NP and 3-NP) existed in the environment are introduced in CDs solutions. The corresponding fluorescence response is shown in Fig. S4 . Obviously, only CDs reacting with 4-NP shows an apparent intensity change while other interference substances and aromatic compounds present negligible influences toward CDs. The above results further indicate the good selectivity and credibility of our method for sensing 4-NP in practical samples.

From the previous literature, most of probes have been constructed for detection based on electron transfer (ET), which ascribes to the intense electron-withdrawing characteristic from nitro groups. However, the above experimental results show that nitrophenol compounds with similar electron-deficient property (2-NP and 3-NP), have an insignificant quenching to CDs fluorescence. This suggests that ET can be excluded as the primary sensing mechanism (Han et al., 2017). Therefore, to explore the response principle of 4-NP in detail, we have systematically performed a series of measurements. Fig. 2 (g) exhibits the UV–vis absorbance of CDs, 4-NP, CDs/4-NP compound solution and theoretical curve of CDs + 4-NP. In the presence of 4-NP, the absorption spectrum of CDs/4-NP nearly keeps in accord with the theoretical curve of CDs + 4-NP, which indicates that no new substance forms between CDs and 4-NP. Further, the zeta potentials are −8.24 mV and −28.02 mV for CDs and 4-NP in the pH = 9 buffer, respectively. As a result, an electrostatic repulsion exists between 4-NP and CDs ( Fig. S5 ), impeding the process of ET. Also, this electrostatic repulsion enables to lengthen the distance between 4-NP and CDs, which disturbs the formation of an efficient FRET donor–acceptor pair (Wang & Zheng, 2022). Therefore, ET and FRET are not the predominating role of response mechanism in this system. Subsequently, we investigate the UV–vis absorption spectrum of 4-NP and the fluorescence spectrum of CDs. As recorded in Fig. 2 (h), the absorption spectrum of 4-NP at 399 nm overlaps largely with the excitation spectrum of CDs at 401 nm. It signals that IFE may be the dominant factor of fluorescence quenching. Simultaneously, the average lifetime of CDs is inappreciably changed in the presence of 4-NP, thereby verifying the IFE-induced quenching effect between CDs and 4-NP (Fig. 2 (i)). Additionally, from the abovementioned analysis (Fig. 2 (a)), the quenching efficiency of the sensing system is insensitive to the temperature variation, further proving that the primary sensing principle is IFE (Ling et al., 2014). As for the similar nitrophenol compounds (2-NP and 3-NP) causing a little influence on fluorescence intensity, it is reasonable to be interpreted as the small overlap area between the absorption bands of 2-NP and 3-NP and the excitation spectrum of CDs ( Fig. S6 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of pH-modulated fluorescence switching detection system
ce:section.ce:para:
Through the above measurements, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs response to 4-NP is rationally elucidated to be IFE. Interestingly, the quenching efficiency is obviously differentiated in an acid/alkali environment. In the 3D fluorescence spectrum of CDs (Fig. 3
(a)), there is one primary area corresponding to the excitation and emission of 401 nm and 470 nm. The absorbance of 4-NP overlaps poorly with the excitation of the CDs at pH = 5 aqueous solution. Thus, the fluorescence variation of CDs is not apparent regardless of adding 4-NP. However, at pH = 7, the intensity of the absorption peak at 399 nm increases, whereas the absorption peak at 320 nm drops. When the pH value of the solution is further modulated to 9, the maximum absorption peak of 4-NP appears at about 399 nm together with the enhancement of intensity. It dramatically increases the overlap area of spectra and enhances the IFE effect, thereby resulting in the PL quenching effect.

In addition, the sensitivity and selectivity of nano-sensors toward 4-NP not only depend on the pH-dominated IFE relationship but also may associate with some interactions between CDs and 4-NP. According to the XPS and FT-IR measurements, these results indicate that the CDs surface is rich in hydrophilic groups (–NH2, –OH and –COOH), while there are some hydroxyl and amino groups in 4-NP molecules. Hydrogen bonds will be advantageously formed as soon as the two meet. Subsequently, we explain this speculation using the binding constant (K). As exhibited in Fig. S7 , the binding constant (K) of CDs is 4.47 × 103 M−1 upon the addition of 4-NP, which is calculated from the Benesi-Hildebrand equation (Yang et al., 2022):
(3)
1 /
( I -
I 0
)
= 1 /
[ K
(
I
max
-
I 0
)
C ]
+ 1 /
(
I
max
-
I 0
)
where I0 , I and Imax are the PL intensities without 4-NP, in the existence of 4-NP at an instant concentration and a saturated concentration, respectively. C denotes the 4-NP concentration. These facts manifest that CDs enable to form the intensive hydrogen bonding to target to 4-NP with the high selectivity, further restricting the absorption of energy and quenching PL intensity. Therefore, all above results support that the detection system between CDs and 4-NP is dramatically regulated by pH and has a notable hydrogen bond interaction.

To better elucidate this pH-modulated PL quenching mechanism of CDs in the existence of 4-NP, UV–vis absorption spectra of 4-NP at different pH levels are collected. As displayed in Fig. 3 (b), the absorption peak at 399 nm gradually declines but the short-wavelength absorption peak at 320 nm enhances as the pH decreases. Since the strong electron-withdrawing effect of the nitro-group makes 4-NP a weak acid with a pKa 7.12, the reversible protonation and deprotonation of phenolic hydroxyl group lead to pH dependency of CDs/4-NP fluorescence (Hua et al., 2017). To be specific, under acidic conditions, 4-NP exists as a molecule state and has a weak quenching effect on CDs. However, under alkaline conditions, the fluorescence of CDs is quenched in response to 4-NP. This is because that the H+ from hydroxyl of 4-NP can be easily caught by OH–, which leads 4-NP to change from molecule state to the oxygen anion state, thus causing the conjugated degree increasement and the absorbance red shift (Qu et al., 2017; Johnson et al., 2013). Consequently, the fluorescence quenching of the system is at a low efficiency when the pH level is less than 6 (Fig. 3 (c)). Moreover, the PL intensity difference (F0 -F) reaches a crest value as the pH level is up to 9. In addition, by modulating the pH level, the luminescence switching of the detection system can be repeated between pH = 5 and pH = 9 for five consecutive cycles without fatigue (Fig. 3 (d)). The intrinsic pH-modulated fluorescence switching mechanism diagram is shown in Fig. 3 (e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs/PVA nanocomposite and smartphone-assisted analysis device
ce:section.ce:para: Given the quick response, sensitivity and simplicity of the proposed method, we establish the smartphone-assisted analysis device for visual 4-NP determination, which equips with the CDs composite film and sample embedding platform. As shown in Scheme 1, primarily, CDs are embedded into PVA to synthesize a visual fluorescence film sensor. And then, the CDs/PVA film is coupled to the modified CWPU to construct a new composite film. This strategy formulates two areas: the detection area (CDs/PVA film) and the hydrophobic area (the modified CWPU film). Especially, the hydrophobic area is made up of the modified CWPU, which has excellent thermal stability, chemical resistance, storage stability, mechanical properties and water resistance (Wang & Huang, 2022). Excellent hydrophobicity enables the testing liquid to be gathered from all around towards the hydrophilic starlike film to improve the accuracy, facilitating the analysis device for application in practice. Furthermore, a sample embedding platform is equipped with a UV light source and a HUAWEI P40 smartphone, which takes advantage of the powerful digital imaging from smartphone. Moreover, darkroom box is employed to reduce the interference from the external light source ( Fig. S8 ). Finally, the smartphone-assisted analysis device has been developed successfully and further devoted to 4-NP sensing (Details of the mechanistic explanation of this device can be found in the supporting information). During the detection process ( Video S1 ), 100 μL 4-NP solutions at various concentrations are dripped into the starlike films. After letting the films stand for 2 min, transfer the films absorbing various doses of 4-NP to the sample sink of the black box, and use the camera of smartphone to obtain the fluorescence images and the application called “Color Picker” to convert the fluorescence color into the corresponding color information (RGB value). According to the CDs fluorescence response to 4-NP at different concentrations, we select the sum RGB values as the ordinate and the 4-NP concentration as the abscissa to build a good linear correlation (RGB value = −4.5502C4-NP + 393.2265, where C4-NP is the concentration of 4-NP), with a LOD of 0.110 μM within the concentration range of 0.110–60 μM and LOQ of 0.350 μM ( Fig. S9 and Tables S5-S6). These results fully demonstrate that this smartphone-assisted analysis device provides a powerful tool for the visually quantitative 4-NP determination in samples.

Furthermore, the accuracy and practicability of smartphone-assisted analysis device for the determination of 4-NP are evaluated. As shown in Fig. 4 , several crops are selected as practical samples encountered in daily life for detection. After spraying 4-NP at several particular concentrations (10 μM, 30 μM and 60 μM) onto the surface of crops (potatoes, cherry tomatoes and apples), the CDs/PVA films are attached to the crops and stand for 2 min. And then take them into the proposed device for the analysis of 4-NP residue. The satisfied recovery rates of 101.6–108.6% are collected in Table S7 . These promising results demonstrate that the developed composite sensor can be suggested as a potential strategy for monitoring the overuse of 4-NP in crop analysis to ensure food safety. Moreover, to the best of our knowledge, CDs/PVA composite film-based analytical smartphone device for detecting 4-NP has not been reported.

In summary, we have prepared eco-friendly CDs derived from bell pepper emitting blue light with a high FQY of 23.2%. Due to efficient IFE between CDs and 4-NP, CDs can achieve a wide range of 4-NP detection range from 0.054 to 68 µM under the alkaline condition, with a low detection limit of 54 nM. Meaningfully, through simply modulating the pH level of solution, the absorption spectrum of 4-NP and hydrogen bonding interaction between CDs and 4-NP suffer from transformation. These induce the occurrence of obvious differential variations in the fluorescence intensity in acidic and alkaline conditions. Thus, the fluorescence state of the reaction system is dramatically regulated by pH, realizing the construction of a pH-modulated fluorescence switching nano-sensor for 4-NP sensing. In addition, the practicability of this nano-sensor is verified by the satisfied recovery rates of 101.8–107.5%. Besides, we design the CDs/PVA composite film containing detection (CDs/PVA film) and hydrophobic (the modified CWPU) areas, and further combine the composite film with the smartphone-assisted device to realize the on-site 4-NP residue analysis. Particularly, the 4-NP residues in crops samples are rapidly detected on the spot with satisfactory recovery rates. To our knowledge, this is the first report about the determination of 4-NP using a sensing system constructed by a specific composite film and smartphone device. This study presents an eco-friendly strategy about the preparation of CDs, and highlights the potential use of CDs for visual detection of environmental pollutants in the future.

Guoqiang Guo: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Software, Writing – original draft. Tingting Li: Formal analysis, Methodology, Software. Ziyi Liu: Data curation, Investigation, Methodology. Xinyu Luo: Visualization, Investigation, Supervision. Ting Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision. Siyuan Tang: Visualization, Investigation. Xu Wang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision. Da Chen: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by projects from National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (No. 61604084) and K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University. The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.137232.
